steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The blank appears in a noun phrase: Lola Álvarez Bravo's photograph [blank] 'The Freeloaders'. The title directly follows the noun to identify which specific photograph is being discussed.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
Structure: "Lola Alvarez Bravo's [blank] 'The Freeloaders' offers a glimpse..." The title 'The Freeloaders' is a restrictive identifier for a specific photograph—it tells us which photograph.

Rule: a restrictive identifier (one that specifies which one) takes no punctuation before it.

A. photograph, — a comma before 'The Freeloaders' would make the title a non-restrictive appositive, implying there are other photographs and this title is just extra information. But the title uniquely identifies this one work. Eliminated.

B. photograph — no punctuation lets 'The Freeloaders' serve as a restrictive title directly attached to the noun. Correct.

C. photograph; — a semicolon separates independent clauses. 'The Freeloaders' is not a clause; placing a semicolon here makes the title a fragment. Eliminated.

D. photograph: — a colon introduces a list or elaboration after an independent clause. The sentence before the blank is not a complete independent clause ("Lola Alvarez Bravo's photograph" is just a noun phrase). Eliminated.

B is correct.
